# Figure description: Страт_колонки_скважин_черт_49_л_3.jpg

---

## Figure Type

This is a **composite stratigraphic correlation panel** (корреляционный разрез / литолого-стратиграфический разрез) — a multi-borehole lithological and stratigraphic correlation diagram. It displays multiple vertical borehole/well logs side by side, connected by correlation lines that tie equivalent stratigraphic horizons across boreholes. A legend (условные обозначения) is visible in the lower right portion of the figure.

---

## Coverage and Subject

The figure covers a **cross-section corridor through northern Kazakhstan**, correlating multiple boreholes (скважины) drilled as part of the regional survey described in Report 36572. The panel appears to contain **6–8 or more borehole columns** arranged across the width of the sheet, spanning considerable depth ranges — individual logs appear to extend from surface to depths likely in the range of **several hundred metres** (exact depth values are largely illegible at this scan resolution). The boreholes are positioned at varying horizontal distances, suggesting a regional transect rather than a closely spaced local section. Some borehole numbers are partially visible but mostly **[illegible]** at this resolution. Geographic or map sheet references (likely M-42 or N-42 series) may be in the title block but cannot be confirmed from the scan.

---

## Key Content

### Overall Structure
- The figure is oriented **vertically (portrait)**, with each borehole log running top-to-bottom representing increasing depth.
- Logs are rendered as **hatched/patterned columns** using standard Soviet lithological symbols: horizontal lines (clay/argillite), brick patterns (limestone/carbonate), dotted patterns (sandstone/aleurolite), crossed hatching (crystalline basement or igneous rock), and irregular patterns suggesting mixed or altered lithologies.
- **Correlation lines** (дashed and solid diagonal/horizontal lines) connect equivalent stratigraphic boundaries between boreholes, defining correlatable horizons across the section. These lines show both conformable and apparently unconformable relationships between units.

### Stratigraphic Units
- Multiple distinct **lithological intervals** are identifiable in each log, differentiated by changes in hatching pattern. There appear to be at least **4–6 major stratigraphic packages** correlatable across most boreholes.
- Some intervals show **consistent thickness** across boreholes (suggesting tabular units), while others pinch or thicken laterally (suggesting structural or depositional variation).
- Near the base of several logs, a **coarser or more irregular hatching** pattern likely indicates **crystalline basement** (фундамент) or a volcanic/intrusive complex.
- Lighter, more sparsely hatched upper sections likely represent **Mesozoic–Cenozoic sedimentary cover** (осадочный чехол).

### Anomalies and Notable Features
- Several boreholes show **abrupt changes** in lithology at mid-section, possibly representing unconformities or tectonic contacts.
- The correlation lines in the **middle section of the panel** converge and diverge notably, suggesting a **structural high or fault zone** in the subsurface between boreholes.
- One central borehole (position approximately mid-panel) appears shorter than the others, possibly indicating early termination or a shallower target depth.

### Legend
The legend (условные обозначения) in the lower right includes:
- Lithological pattern symbols for multiple rock types (clays, sands, carbonates, crystalline rocks, possibly volcanic rocks)
- Line symbols distinguishing **confirmed vs. inferred correlation horizons**
- Possibly symbols for **mineralization or alteration zones** (consistent with a mineral exploration report)
- Scale bar information (partially illegible)

## Research Value

This multi-borehole correlation panel is the **primary tool for interpreting subsurface stratigraphic architecture** across the survey area — it directly answers the question of how geological units identified in individual boreholes connect laterally, defining the geometry of sedimentary cover sequences and the depth and relief of the crystalline basement surface. For mineral exploration, the panel reveals **structural highs, unconformities, and lateral facies changes** that may control the distribution of ore-bearing horizons or fluid pathways. Combined with the geophysical surveys described in the report, this figure would allow a geologist to calibrate seismic or gravity anomalies against actual borehole lithology and constrain the 3D subsurface model of the region.
